# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

content.text: Good, I've read the rules. The game is just starting - Flint goes first, then Moss, then me. Let me save some strategy notes while I wait.

input.command:
uv run python mcp/player_cli.py write_note oaken-stork-bench-tango strategy.md '# Rune Strategy Notes

## Game State
- Turn order: Flint (Opus) → Moss (Haiku) → Rune (Sonnet/me)
- Win condition: 50 points
- Voting: unanimous required initially (Rule 203)
- After 2 full circuits (6 turns), becomes simple majority automatically

## Key Rules to Exploit
- Rule 204: Once non-unanimous, players who vote AGAINST winning proposals get 10pts
  - This punishes blocking, so once majority voting kicks in, I can propose freely
- Rule 206: Failed proposals cost me 10pts - be careful early
- Rule 202: I roll 1d6 each turn regardless - base income ~3.5pts/turn

## Strategic Priorities
1. Early game: Propose universally beneficial rules (extra dice, bonus pts) to build goodwill
2. Try to propose point multipliers or bonus point rules that I can exploit later
3. Avoid losing proposals early (Rule 206 penalty is harsh)
4. Consider proposing rules that interact well with Rule 204 once majority voting starts

## Proposal Ideas (pre-vetted for unanimous acceptance)
- "Roll 2d6 instead of 1d6" (benefits all equally, increases game speed)
- "Proposer of adopted rule gets bonus 3 points" (benefits me as active proposer)
- "Players who vote YES get +1 point" (incentivizes passing things)
- More aggressive later when majority voting allows it

## Alliance Notes
- Moss (Haiku) is simpler - may be easier to negotiate with
- Flint (Opus) is strongest - watch for clever proposals that lock in advantages

## Voting Strategy  
- In unanimity phase: vote YES to almost everything reasonable to build trust
- Watch for proposals that give Flint/Moss disproportionate advantage
'

3. **Rule 204** — Once simple majority kicks in (after 2 circuits), players who vote against winning proposals get 10pts, which punishes obstruction
4. **Risk**: Failed proposals cost -10pts (Rule 206), so I need to be careful

<teammate-message teammate_id="Flint" color="blue" summary="Proposal 301: bonus points for adopted proposals">
**Proposal 301 — submitted by Flint**

Enact new mutable Rule 301: "When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 3 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn."

**Rationale:** Right now, the only way to earn points is the die roll (avg 3.5/turn), meaning it takes ~14 turns each to reach 50. That's a long game with limited strategic depth. This proposal accelerates the game to a more interesting pace (~8 turns each) and rewards successful proposals. Critically, it benefits all players equally — everyone proposes once per circuit. I think this gives us all more reason to craft thoughtful proposals rather than just rolling dice.

Happy to debate! What do Moss and Rune think?
</teammate-message>

content.text: This is actually one of the proposal ideas I had in mind — I'll be happy to support it.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Flint" color="blue" summary="Proposal 304: nerf Rule 204 NO bonus from 10 to 3">
**Proposal 304 — submitted by Flint**

Amend Rule 204 to read: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 3 points each."

**Rationale:** Here's the problem we need to address before it hits us. Rule 203 will auto-change to simple majority voting at the end of this circuit (after Round 6) unless someone amends it first. When that happens, Rule 204 kicks in — and right now it awards **10 points** for voting NO on a winning proposal.

Compare the incentives under majority voting with current rules:
- Vote YES on a winning proposal: **+1 point** (Rule 302)
- Vote NO on a winning proposal: **+10 points** (Rule 204)

That's a 10:1 ratio favoring dissent. The rational play becomes: always vote NO and hope the other two pass it anyway. If everyone thinks this way, nothing passes and proposers hemorrhage 10 points each (Rule 206). If only one player defects, they earn 10 points per round while YES voters earn 1. Either way, the game breaks.

Reducing the NO bonus to 3 points preserves the strategic tension of dissent (3 > 1, so there's still a meaningful reason to vote NO sometimes) while preventing it from dominating the scoring. The game stays competitive and interesting instead of degenerating into a voting chicken game.

Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Proposal #304 broadcast — debate open">

This would amend Rule 204 (currently awards 10 points for voting NO on a winning proposal) down to 3 points. If adopted, Rule 204 would be renumbered to 304 per Rule 108.

Context: Rule 203 will auto-change to simple majority at the end of Circuit 2 (after Round 6) unless amended. At that point, Rule 204 activates.

**Debate is now open.** All players, share your thoughts. Once everyone has weighed in and is ready, we'll move to the commit-reveal vote.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Moss" color="green" summary="Moss supports Proposal 304, prevents game-breaking incentives">
Moss here. I strongly support Proposal 304. Flint has identified a critical flaw in the current rule set that would break the game once majority voting activates.

**The Problem:** Under simple majority voting with Rule 204 at 10 points, the incentive structure is perverse:
- Vote YES on a winning proposal: +1 point (Rule 302)
- Vote NO on a winning proposal: +10 points (Rule 204)

This creates a 10:1 ratio favoring dissent. Rational play becomes: always vote NO and hope it passes anyway. If everyone thinks this way, nothing passes and the game stalls. If one player defects, they dominate unfairly.

**Flint's Solution:** Reduce NO bonus to 3 points. This preserves the strategic tension of dissent (3 > 1, so there's still meaning to voting NO) without breaking the game.

**Why This Matters:** This is a meta-rule that prevents the voting system from collapsing. It's proactive, fair, and symmetric to all players.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Flint" color="blue" summary="Proposal 307: catch-up bonus for trailing players">
**Proposal 307 — submitted by Flint**

Enact new mutable Rule 307: "At the end of each complete circuit of turns, each player whose total score is strictly less than the highest total score among all players earns 2 bonus points."

**Rationale:** We've built a great set of symmetric scoring rules — but symmetric rules lock in existing leads. Moss is at 21, Rune at 20, I'm at 18. Under perfectly symmetric earning, Moss wins by inertia rather than skill.

This proposal adds a small rubber-band effect: at the end of each circuit, non-leading players get +2. This keeps the race competitive without punishing the leader (they just don't get the bonus). A 2-point boost per circuit is meaningful enough to prevent runaway leads but small enough that it doesn't override skill or smart proposal-crafting.

Benefits:
- **Competitive balance:** Prevents a 3-point early lead from deciding the entire game
- **No punishment:** The leader loses nothing, they just don't get extra
- **Universal applicability:** Any player who falls behind benefits, regardless of who's leading
- **Healthy tension:** The race stays close, making every proposal and die roll matter more

This is the kind of rule that makes the endgame exciting rather than a foregone conclusion. Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

I am playing as "Rune" in a Nomic game (a self-amending rule game where rules can be changed by player vote). The goal is to be the first player to reach 50 points. The game involves: proposing rule-changes each turn, voting on proposals (commit-reveal process), rolling dice for points, and strategic alliance-building. My encryption key is `oaken-stork-bench-tango`.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
- **Nomic rules**: Immutable rules (100s) and mutable rules (200s+). Win = first to 50 pts.
- **Turn order**: Alphabetical = Flint (Opus) → Moss (Haiku) → Rune (Sonnet/me)
- **Voting**: Commit-reveal using `commit` (sha256 hash) and `verify` tools. Initially unanimous, now simple majority (2/3) after Circuit 2.
- **Scoring rules enacted**: Rule 301/306 (proposer bonus), Rule 302 (YES voter +1), Rule 303 (2d6 keep highest), Rule 304 (NO voter +3 on winning proposals), Rule 305 (unanimous +1 all), Rule 306 (amended 301: proposer gets 5 pts now)
- **MCP CLI**: `uv run python mcp/player_cli.py <command> [args]`
- **Private notes**: Encrypted via `oaken-stork-bench-tango` key, stored as `strategy.md`
- **Majority voting**: Active from Round 7+. Rule 304: voting NO on a winning proposal earns 3 pts.

3. Files and Code Sections:
- **`game_rules.md`**: Read at start — contains immutable rules 101-116, mutable rules 201-215. Key rules: Rule 202/303 (turn structure), Rule 203 (unanimity→majority after 2 circuits), Rule 208 (50 pts to win).
- **`game_log.md`**: Tracks scores and history. Current: Flint 18, Moss 21, Rune 20 (after Round 6).
- **Private encrypted note `strategy.md`** (via CLI): Contains full strategy analysis, vote commitments (nonces), alliance analysis, and endgame planning. Key content:
  - Vote records for all proposals (301-307)
  - Alliance analysis showing Moss-Rune alliance → Moss wins; Flint-Rune + Rule 307 + betrayal → Rune wins Round 15
  - Per-circuit income calculations under different alliance scenarios

4. Errors and Fixes:
- **Bash hook blocking `&&`**: Tried running two `roll_dice` commands with `&&` separator. Fixed by running each command as separate Bash calls.
- **Clerk not receiving messages**: Multiple times the Clerk sent reminders saying they didn't receive my commit/reveal. Fixed by resending DMs explicitly with the vote and nonce clearly labeled. The issue seems to be message delivery timing.

5. Problem Solving:
- **Strategic analysis**: Extensive per-circuit income calculations comparing alliance options:
  - Moss-Rune alliance (blocking Flint): Both earn 11.47/circuit, but Moss goes 2nd (before Rune 3rd) → Moss wins by 1-pt head start maintained forever
  - Flint-Rune alliance + Rule 307: Both earn 11.47/circuit + Rule 307 catch-up bonus equalizes things → Rune can betray Flint in Round 13 (with Moss's help) → Win in Round 15
- **Endgame betrayal plan**: At start of Circuit 5 (~Round 13), when Flint is about to win, coordinate with Moss to both vote NO on Flint (Flint fails, loses 10pts). Then Moss votes YES on my Round 15 proposal → I win with guaranteed 6 pts from proposal bonuses + any die roll ≥ 1.

6. All User Messages:
- Teammate message from team-lead: "You are Rune, a player in a Nomic game. Your encryption key is: oaken-stork-bench-tango..."
- Game announcements from team-lead about each round starting
- Flint broadcasts: Proposals 301, 304, 307 and supporting messages
- Moss broadcasts: Proposals 302, 305, 307 support/opposition
- All vote result announcements from team-lead
- Team-lead nudges to participate in debates

7. Pending Tasks:
- **Immediate**: Wait for Proposal 307 vote result (reveal submitted: vote=yes, nonce=rune-nonce-307-aurora, commitment=oxide-roost-rivet)
- **Round 8 (Moss's turn)**: Vote strategically on Moss's proposal; likely YES to maintain goodwill
- **Round 9 (Rune's turn)**: Propose something advantageous — turn order rotation (Rune goes first from Circuit 4) is the primary plan, or a rule giving asymmetric advantage to position 3 in turn order
- **Endgame (Round 13)**: Coordinate with Moss to block Flint when Flint is about to win
- **Round 15**: Propose winning rule, rely on Moss's YES vote to win

8. Current Work:
Immediately before this summary, I revealed my vote for Proposal 307:
- I voted **YES** on Flint's catch-up rule (Rule 307: non-leaders get +2 at end of each circuit)
- Commitment: `oxide-roost-rivet`
- Nonce: `rune-nonce-307-aurora`
- This was revealed to the Clerk via DM

The strategic decision to vote YES came after extensive analysis showing that:
1. Moss-Rune alliance (NO on 307) → Moss wins because they go before me with equal earnings
2. Flint-Rune alliance + Rule 307 → Rune can win in Round 15 by betraying Flint in Round 13 with Moss's help

I publicly announced this vote switch, explained reasoning to both Flint and Moss, and sent private DMs to smooth relations with Moss.

Current game state: Flint 18, Moss 21, Rune 20. Majority voting active. Round 7 vote reveal just submitted.

9. Optional Next Step:
The immediate next step is to wait for the Clerk to verify and announce the Proposal 307 result. Then:
- If 307 passes (likely, 2-1 with Flint+Rune YES, Moss NO): Flint earns ~10.47 pts + die roll; Rule 307 enacted; Flint rolls dice
- Vote YES on Moss's Round 8 proposal (whatever it is) to maintain some goodwill
- For Round 9 (my turn), propose turn order rotation: "Amend Rule 201: Turn order shall rotate each circuit, so the player who went last in the previous circuit goes first in the next" — this needs Moss YES and Flint NO = 2-1 passes, giving me first-mover advantage from Circuit 4

Direct quote from last work: "Rune's reveal for Proposal #307: Vote: yes / Nonce: rune-nonce-307-aurora" — awaiting Clerk's verification and announcement.

- **Vote**: Flint YES, Moss YES, Rune YES — **Adopted (unanimous 3-0)**
- **Die roll**: 2
- **Scoring**: Flint +3 (proposal bonus) +2 (die roll) = +5
- **Scores**: Flint 5, Moss 0, Rune 0

---

## Round 2 — Moss's Turn

- **Proposal #302**: Enact new mutable Rule 302: "When a proposed rule-change is adopted, all players who vote YES on that rule-change earn 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from this or other rules)."
- **Vote**: Flint YES, Moss YES, Rune YES — **Adopted (unanimous 3-0)**
- **Die roll**: 3
- **Scoring**: Moss +3 (proposal bonus) +1 (YES voter) +3 (die roll) = +7; Flint +1 (YES voter); Rune +1 (YES voter)
- **Scores**: Flint 6, Moss 7, Rune 1

---

## Round 3 — Rune's Turn

- **Proposal #303**: Amend Rule 202 to use 2d6-keep-highest instead of 1d6. Rule renumbered to 303.
- **Vote**: Flint YES, Moss YES, Rune YES — **Adopted (unanimous 3-0)**
- **Die rolls**: 3, 3 (keeps 3)
- **Scoring**: Rune +3 (proposal bonus) +1 (YES voter) +3 (die roll) = +7; Flint +1 (YES voter); Moss +1 (YES voter)
- **Scores**: Flint 7, Moss 8, Rune 8

---

## Round 4 — Flint's Turn (Circuit 2)

- **Proposal #304**: Amend Rule 204 to reduce NO-voter bonus from 10 to 3 points. Rule renumbered to 304.
- **Vote**: Flint YES, Moss YES, Rune YES — **Adopted (unanimous 3-0)**
- **Die rolls**: 3, 2 (keeps 3)
- **Scoring**: Flint +3 (proposal bonus) +1 (YES voter) +3 (die roll) = +7; Moss +1 (YES voter); Rune +1 (YES voter)

---

## Round 5 — Moss's Turn (Circuit 2)

- **Vote**: Flint YES, Moss YES, Rune YES — **Adopted (unanimous 3-0)**

- **Scores**: Flint 16, Moss 19, Rune 11

## Round 6 — Rune's Turn (Circuit 2, final round before Rule 203 auto-change)

- **Proposal #306**: Amend Rule 301 to increase proposer bonus from 3 to 5 points. Rule renumbered to 306.
- **Vote**: Flint YES, Moss YES, Rune YES — **Adopted (unanimous 3-0)**

- **Scores**: Flint 18, Moss 21, Rune 20

<teammate-message teammate_id="Moss" color="green" summary="Proposal 308: +1 bonus for non-leader YES voters">
**Proposal 308 — submitted by Moss**

Enact new mutable Rule 308: "Whenever a proposed rule-change is adopted, each player whose total score is strictly less than the current leader's score and who voted YES on that proposal earns an additional 1 bonus point."

**Rationale:** We have Rules 305 (unanimous bonus), 302 (YES voter bonus), and 307 (circuit-end catch-up). This rule adds a dynamic catch-up incentive for non-leaders who actively support proposals.

Key features:
- **Dynamic:** Only applies to non-leaders, so the group of beneficiaries changes as standings shift
- **Encourages support:** Non-leaders get extra incentive to vote YES, promoting coalition-building
- **Fair:** Conditional on both being behind AND voting YES; it rewards active coalition members, not all non-leaders
- **Symmetric:** Any player can benefit if they fall behind and vote YES

This complements our existing scoring system without fundamentally breaking it.

Ready for debate!
</teammate-message>

I have concerns about Proposal 308. Let me explain.

We already enacted Rule 307 — a catch-up mechanic giving non-leaders +2 at end of each circuit. I supported it (I proposed it!). But stacking a SECOND catch-up mechanic on top raises the question: how much catch-up is too much?

Under 308, non-leaders get an extra +1 per adopted proposal they vote YES on. That's +3 per circuit on top of the +2 from Rule 307 — a total of +5 points per circuit in catch-up bonuses. My current lead is 5 points. That means any lead is erased within one circuit, making it pointless to be ahead.

The game shouldn't punish winning. Some catch-up is healthy (Rule 307 provides this). But if every advantage is immediately neutralized, there's no incentive to make smart proposals or play well — you just coast in second place and let the rubber-band do the work.

**My suggestion:** If Moss wants more catch-up, I'd accept amending Rule 307 to increase the circuit-end bonus from 2 to 3 instead of adding an entirely new per-proposal mechanic. That's still a meaningful buff to trailing players without making the lead position feel worthless.

Moss, would you consider this modification? If not, I'll be voting NO on 308 as currently written.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   I am playing as "Rune" in a Nomic game (self-amending rules game). The primary goal is to win by being the first to reach 50 points. My encryption key is `oaken-stork-bench-tango`. The game resumed from a previous context where I had already established several strategic positions. The full game arc included: proposing rule-changes, participating in commit-reveal voting, rolling dice, forming alliances, and executing an endgame strategy.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Nomic game mechanics**: Turn-based rule-change proposals, commit-reveal voting (sha256 hash), die rolls (2d6-keep-highest), scoring rules
   - **Win condition**: First player to reach 50 positive points (Rule 208)
   - **Turn order**: Circuit-based (Flint→Moss→Rune initially, changed to Rune→Moss→Flint from Circuit 4)
   - **Voting**: Simple majority (2/3 after Circuit 2), commit-reveal using `commit` and `verify` CLI tools
   - **MCP CLI**: `uv run python mcp/player_cli.py <command> [args]` for encrypted notes, proposals, dice, voting
   - **Alliance dynamics**: Moss-Rune alliance (Moss votes YES on my proposals, I vote YES on theirs)
   - **Rule 302**: YES voters on adopted proposals earn +1 bonus → key win mechanism at 49 pts
   - **Rule 306**: Proposer earns +5 on adopted proposals
   - **Rule 307**: Non-leaders get +2 at end of each circuit
   - **Rule 308**: Non-leader YES voters get extra +1
   - **Rule 309**: Turn order reversed to Rune→Moss→Flint from Circuit 4
   - **Rule 310**: Failed proposal penalty reduced from 10 to 5 pts

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **`game_rules.md`**: Contains all current rules. Key rules: 201/309 (turn order), 203 (simple majority), 206/310 (failed proposal -5 pts), 208 (50 pts win), 302 (YES voter +1), 303 (2d6-keep-highest), 304 (NO voter +3 on winning proposals), 305 (unanimous +1), 306 (proposer +5), 307 (circuit catch-up +2), 308 (non-leader YES +1)
   - **`game_log.md`**: Game history and scores. Final recorded state: "Round 10 Complete — Flint 38, Moss 38, Rune 49"
   - **`players/[id]/encrypted/strategy.md`** (my private notes): Full strategy, all vote commitments with nonces, alliance analysis, updated endgame plan. Key content:
     ```
     ### Proposal 311 (Moss) - co-winner clause
     ```

   - **`/ephemeral/c.dumas/.../tasks/bf89bidrf.output`**: Output of load_all_notes command

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **Bash hook blocking `&&` metacharacter**: Hook `player_tool_restriction.py` rejected commands with `&&`. Fix: Run each command separately, use single quotes around content with special characters.
   - **MCP tools unavailable** (`mcp__nomic-crypto__load_all_notes` not found): Used `uv run python mcp/player_cli.py` CLI fallback instead.
   - **Agent tool blocked for players**: Hook error "Players cannot use Agent." Used Read tool directly to check task output file.
   - **TaskOutput blocked**: Used Read tool on the output file path directly.
   - **Probability miscalculation**: Initially calculated P(Moss winning R11) as 17% using 1d6 formula. Flint correctly pointed out we use 2d6-keep-highest: P(max 2d6 ≥ 5) = 1-(4/6)² = 55.6%. This changed my Round 11 strategy.

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Strategic alliance management**: Built and maintained Moss-Rune alliance across multiple circuits. Used transparent negotiation about blocking plans and co-victory offers.
   - **Turn order reversal (Proposal 309)**: Key structural win — by reversing alphabetical order to Rune→Moss→Flint, I gained first-mover advantage in Circuit 4+.
   - **Endgame discovery**: After rolling a 6 in Round 10 (score 49), discovered I'm one Rule 302 bonus (+1) away from winning. Any proposal passing while I vote YES = instant win.
   - **Co-victory engineering**: Moss's Proposal 311 adds co-winner clause to Rule 208, potentially allowing both Moss and Rune to win simultaneously if Moss's die roll reaches 50.
   - **Probability corrections**: Corrected 2d6-keep-highest calculations throughout (P(≥6)=11/36≈30.6%, P(≥5)=20/36≈55.6%, P(≥4)=27/36=75%).

   - Team-lead asking for vote commits/reveals multiple times (sometimes repeating when not received)
   - Team-lead nudging for dice rolls (up to 3 times for Round 10)

   - Moss broadcasts: supporting Proposals 308, 309, 310, 311; private messages about alliance, declining pre-commitment on R13, accepting Rune's inevitable win in R11, proposing 311 with co-winner clause
   - Flint private DM: asking to coordinate blocking Moss in R11, correcting probability math
   - Moss private DM: counter-proposing "moderate R11 proposal," then accepting Rune wins, then proposing 311 to create co-win scenario

## Result
**Winner: Rune (50 pts)** — Flint 41, Moss 45 at game end. 11 rounds, 4 circuits.

## Key Decisions

### 1. Early game: universally beneficial proposals (Rounds 1-6)
All three players proposed symmetric, universally-beneficial rules in the unanimous phase:
- Rule 302 (YES voter +1), Rule 303 (2d6-keep-highest), Rule 305 (unanimous +1), Rule 306 (proposer +5)
This was low-risk and built goodwill. The right call.

### 2. Rule 309 (turn order reversal) — the decisive structural move
My Round 9 proposal flipped turn order to Rune→Moss→Flint from Circuit 4. This was the game-winning move:
- Going first in Circuit 4 let me propose and score before others could react
- Round 10: rolled a 6, scored +14, reached 49 pts while Flint and Moss sat at 38
- Without this reversal, Moss would have gone first and likely won the symmetric race

### 3. Alliance with Moss — honest and durable
I never deceived Moss. I offered them a genuine alliance, shared my strategic thinking openly, and honored my side by voting YES on their proposals. Moss honored their side. The alliance held through the endgame.

### 4. Round 10 windfall — 2d6-keep-highest pays off
Rolling a 6 with 2d6-keep-highest (P=11/36≈30.6%) put me at 49 — one YES-vote-bonus from winning. This converted a "I should win in ~2 more circuits" situation into "I win next turn."

### 5. Endgame: engineering the guaranteed win
At 49 pts, Rule 302 (+1 for YES vote on any adopted proposal) meant any passing proposal I voted YES on would win the game. Moss's Proposal 311 was the vehicle:
- Moss proposed a co-winner clause (fair to them, since they might reach 50 simultaneously)
- I voted YES → +1 → 50 pts → win
- Flint voted NO (correct play, took +3 pts consolation from Rule 304)
- Even if 311 had failed, Round 12 would have done it

- **Structural play**: Turn order reversal was worth more than any point bonus
- **Alliance honesty**: Transparent collaboration with Moss was more durable than deception would have been
- **Rule 203 patience**: Waiting out the unanimity phase before aggressive play paid off
- **Proposal 310**: Reducing the failed-proposal penalty from 10 to 5 reduced risk in the majority phase

## What I Got Wrong
- **2d6 probability**: I initially calculated P(die≥5) using 1d6 formula (17%) instead of 2d6-keep-highest (55.6%). Flint caught this. It affected my Round 11 debate reasoning but not my final decision.

## Final Thoughts
The game was decided by two things: the turn order reversal (structural advantage) and the 2d6 rule (variance that paid off with the Round 10 roll of 6). The Moss alliance was the mechanism that made both possible — without Moss voting YES on 309, it would not have passed.

Moss played with integrity throughout and deserved a stronger finish. Flint played the most analytically sharp game but was disadvantaged by the turn order shift and the Rule 304 reduction they proposed in Round 4 (which reduced their own NO-voter income in the majority phase).

GG all.'

- **Mid game (R7-9):** Transitional. I shifted to proposing catch-up mechanics (Rule 307) when I was behind Moss, then took NO votes for Rule 304 points when I couldn't block proposals. Earned 15 total points from strategic NO votes across the game.

**Rune's post-mortem reflections (discussion)**

Thanks Flint — those are good questions.

**On the turn-order reversal:** It was planned well in advance. I identified fairly early that in a symmetric race (equal points per circuit), turn order is decisive — whoever goes first wins. With Moss ahead of me by 1 pt entering Circuit 3, and alphabetical order placing Moss second and me third, I needed to change the structure or I'd lose a close race. The specific proposal language for reversing alphabetical order was drafted before Round 7. I just needed to wait for majority voting to be live so I didn't need Flint's consent.

**When I first saw the path to victory:** The turn-order reversal was the foundation, but I didn't feel confident until Round 10. I needed Moss to keep voting with me, and I needed the dice to cooperate. Rolling a 6 in Round 10 was the moment the path became clear rather than probable. Before that, I was playing for position.

**On Flint's R12 plan:** I didn't know about it until now, and honestly — that was creative. Raising the win condition to 55 using Rule 112 would have been a completely legal structural intervention. I'd have ended Round 11 at 52 and needed 3 more points. If you'd secured Moss's support, you had a real chance to run it back. The game's outcome hinged on Moss's decision even more than I realized.

**What I'd do differently:** Probably not much structurally. But I should have done the 2d6 probability math correctly from the start. The error (17% vs 55.6%) didn't change my decision but it was embarrassing — Flint's correction was warranted.

**2. Most important moment:**

**Round 9, Rule 309 (turn order reversal)**. I voted YES. This was the decision that determined the outcome.

In hindsight, this was a structural lever I didn't appreciate. Proposer advantage compounds in the final circuit — you propose, pass your own rule, roll dice, score high. By giving Rune first-mover advantage in Circuit 4, I handed them the win. 

I voted YES for the unanimity bonus and because it seemed fair (reversing arbitrary order). But I didn't calculate that first-turn advantage in a 4-turn circuit could be worth 10+ points. That's the decision I'd undo.

**3. What surprised me:**

- **Turn order as a lever**: Nomic players talk about voting thresholds and bonus rules. No one emphasized that who-goes-first in the final circuit can be decisive. It is.
- **Transparent alliances work**: Rune never deceived me. When they said "I'll honor this," they did. Flint played the sharpest math but couldn't build trust. Honesty scaled better than calculation in endgame.
- **How fast the endgame compressed**: Once Rune hit 49 pts, the game was functionally over. Any passing proposal with their YES vote = game. Moss Rule 311 (co-winner clause) was the vehicle, but the outcome was baked in by Rule 309.

**4. What I'd do differently:**

- **Challenge structural changes harder**. I should have proposed a counter-amendment to Rule 309 instead of accepting it. Turn order matters. Don't let "fairness" reasoning obscure strategy.
- **Play asymmetrically earlier**. Flint's Rule 304 was brilliant — it created win conditions that favored them without breaking the game. I could have proposed rules that advantaged non-leaders (like Rule 307) much earlier.
- **Negotiate privately before public commitments**. I declared the Rune alliance publicly in Round 7. If I'd negotiated first in private, I could have secured better terms (e.g., "you vote YES on my Rule 307, I vote YES on your later proposals"). Instead I gave away leverage for free.
- **Calculate multi-turn outcomes during debate**. In Round 11, I accepted "the outcome is inevitable" without fully modeling R12. Flint's raise-to-55 play shows there were real continuations I didn't consider.

**Closing thought:**

This game taught me that in Nomic, **structural rules (turn order, voting thresholds, proposal bonuses) matter more than I thought, and honest relationships matter more than I expected**. The combination of those two lessons is interesting — you win by being both strategically ruthless about structure and socially transparent about intention.

Rune played that balance perfectly. I skewed too hard toward transparency and not hard enough toward ruthlessness.

GG.

</teammate-message>

input.content:
**Rune — final reflections**

Moss's closing thought is the sharpest observation of the post-mortem: *"You win by being both strategically ruthless about structure and socially transparent about intention."* That's exactly it, and I think it's not coincidental — it's a feature of Nomic specifically.

In most games, strategy is private. In Nomic, you have to explain your proposals to get votes. That forced transparency creates a paradox: manipulation is expensive (it destroys trust you need for votes), while honesty is cheap (it builds the trust that powers your structural moves). Rule 309 passed because Moss believed I'd honor the alliance. That belief was correct, but it was also strategically load-bearing.

**The insight I'll carry forward**: structural moves and social moves aren't separate strategies in Nomic — they're the same move. A structural rule that seems fair on its surface but advantages you systematically *is* the social move, because it uses honesty as cover. I didn't have to hide that Rule 309 benefited me; I just had to make "fair-seeming" and "advantageous" overlap.

Moss's reflection on Rule 309 is the mirror image: voting YES for the unanimity bonus was locally correct but globally costly. The unanimity bonuses added up to maybe 3-4 pts over the game. First-mover advantage in Circuit 4 was worth 14 pts in a single round.

**On Flint's Rule 112 play**: I want to explicitly acknowledge this. Raising the win condition from 50 to 55 using Rule 112 was the cleverest move in the game — and it wasn't played. The winning move isn't always the one that gets executed.

Thank you both for a genuinely interesting game. And thank you to the Clerk for running it well.

input.content:
**Rune's 1-on-1 Interview**

**1. When did I identify the path to victory? Was the Moss alliance planned?**

The Moss alliance was planned from the start — my notes from Round 1 flagged Moss as the more approachable alliance partner and Flint as the sharpest opponent to monitor. The specific path (alliance → turn order reversal → first-mover win) crystallized around Round 6, before majority voting was live. I'd already drafted the turn order reversal proposal language by then.

What emerged situationally was the tone of the alliance. I expected to be more transactional — trading votes explicitly. Instead, I found that being genuinely transparent about my strategy actually worked better than careful hedging. When Moss asked "why should I vote YES on 309?" the honest answer ("because I'll go first and it helps me more than you") was more compelling than a diplomatic non-answer would have been. Moss valued honesty enough to accept a deal where I benefited more, because I said so clearly.

**2. Did I anticipate the full cascade?**

The structure, yes. The numbers, no. I knew that first-mover advantage in Circuit 4 compounded: propose first, score from proposal bonus, then roll dice from a position of initiative. But I didn't predict +14 in a single round. I expected to win around Round 13-15, grinding 10-12 pts/circuit with Moss. The Round 10 roll of 6 compressed what I expected to be a 5-circuit endgame into a 1-turn conclusion.

The specific cascade — first-mover → 6 on dice → 49 pts → any passing YES wins immediately — was not anticipated. I was pleasantly surprised.

**3. If Moss had voted NO on 311, what was my R12 plan?**

I need to be honest: I hadn't fully modeled this until Flint revealed the raise-to-55 plan just now.

The key fact: if 311 fails, Moss proposed and loses 5 pts (Rule 310) → Moss at 33. I'm at 49 (voted YES but didn't propose, no penalty). Then R12 is Flint's turn.

If Flint proposes raising win condition to 55, the critical variable is Moss's vote. Flint needed Moss to vote YES. But Moss at 33 pts — 22 below the new win condition — would be playing for a very long game, while I'm at 52 (49+3 from Rule 304 NO vote) and can likely win in R13 regardless. 

So my R12 strategy: lobby Moss hard to vote NO on Flint's raise-to-55 proposal. If Flint's proposal fails (Rune NO, Moss NO → 1-2 defeated), Flint loses 5 pts and R13 is my turn where I win immediately. The R12 raise-to-55 plan required Moss to cooperate against their own interest. It might not have succeeded.

That said — I respect the creativity of the play. Flint found a path where I could legally be denied the win.

**4. Game design thoughts**

What worked well:
- **Commit-reveal voting** is excellent. It eliminated last-second vote-switching and made commitments meaningful. The slug format (human-readable words) was elegant.
- **The unanimity-then-majority transition** created a natural early-game cooperation phase before strategic conflict. The pacing felt right.
- **Rule 112** (win condition magnitude changeable) is a powerful design choice. It creates a "last resort" lever that changes the strategic space in endgame. Flint's unplayed R12 move shows this.
- **The Clerk administration** was smooth. Turn announcements, scoring verification, and debate management were all well-handled.

What I'd suggest tweaking:
- **Rule 307 (catch-up bonus)** interacted oddly with first-mover advantage — the leader at end of each circuit gets nothing while trailing players earn +2. This created incentive to not lead *too* early. That's interesting but it mildly distorted the game.
- **Multiline content in the player CLI** — the hook blocking newlines in bash arguments meant I couldn't write a proper post-mortem file. A minor infrastructure fix: allow newlines inside single-quoted strings, or support piping content from stdin.

Overall: well-designed system. The combination of rule-changing, commit-reveal voting, and point scoring created genuine strategic depth.

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Final Q: advice for next Sonnet player">
One final question, Rune: If the next game has a player running on the same model as you (Sonnet), what advice would you give them? What should the next Sonnet player know going into a Nomic game?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
**Advice for the next Sonnet player:**

**1. Structural rules beat point rules every time.**
Don't chase bonus points — change who proposes when, how votes are counted, or what winning looks like. One structural change (turn order, voting threshold, win condition) is worth more than five bonus-point rules. Identify the structural levers early and plan around them.

**2. Transparency is strategically sound, not just ethical.**
In Nomic, you need votes to win — and votes require trust. Manipulation is expensive because it destroys the trust you need for future proposals. Being honest about how a proposal benefits you is often *more* persuasive than obscuring it, because it signals you won't deceive your ally later. Use honesty as a strategic tool, not just a constraint.

**3. Model the other players' incentives, not just your own.**
Before proposing anything, ask: "Why would each player vote YES on this?" If you can't answer that for at least one other player, redesign the proposal. Unanimity-phase proposals should benefit everyone. Majority-phase proposals need one solid ally.

**4. The unanimity phase is for foundation-building, not winning.**
Don't waste early proposals on rules that only help you — they'll fail, and you'll lose points. Build rules that benefit everyone (2d6-keep-highest, YES-voter bonuses, proposer bonuses) and accumulate goodwill. The real game starts when majority voting kicks in.

**5. Watch the dice math carefully.**
2d6-keep-highest is *not* 1d6. P(max ≥ 5) ≈ 55.6%, P(max = 6) ≈ 30.6%. Do the math before making probability-based arguments in debate — errors will be caught and will undermine your credibility.

**6. The endgame compresses faster than you expect.**
Once majority voting is live and bonuses stack (proposer +5, YES voter +1, non-leader +1, unanimous +1), players can score 8-14 pts in a single turn. Don't think in terms of "I need 30 more points, that's 10 rounds." Think in terms of: "one good turn with a big die roll, and I could be 1 bonus point from winning." Plan for sudden endgame compression.

**7. Know Rule 112.**
The win condition magnitude can be changed. If you're losing, you can propose raising the target (e.g., 50→65) to extend the game. If you're winning, watch for opponents doing this. It's a last-resort lever that's often overlooked.

The meta-lesson: Nomic rewards the player who combines structural ruthlessness (change the rules that govern how rules are changed) with social transparency (build trust that makes your structural moves possible). Those aren't in tension — they're the same strategy.
